runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 7 in a U.S. microsimulation model
citations: Gruber & Saez (2002) - Elasticity of taxable income estimates
Congressional Budget Office (CBO) microsimulation substitution elasticity assumptions
Immervoll et al. (2007) - EUROMOD/tax-benefit simulation labor supply elasticities
runs.reasoningSummary: For primary earners (typically men or household heads), the labor supply literature generally finds modest substitution elasticities, often in the range of 0.1–0.4 on the intensive margin. Decile 7 represents upper-middle earners who are strongly attached to the labor force, suggesting a moderate but not negligible response. CBO and similar microsimulation models typically calibrate primary-earner substitution elasticities in the 0.15–0.30 range for middle-to-upper deciles. I center my estimate at 0.20, reflecting that decile 7 workers have relatively inelastic labor supply but still respond to marginal wage changes on the intensive margin. Uncertainty spans from near-zero (very inelastic) to about 0.45 (if the model incorporates broader taxable-income responses).

(2007) - EUROMOD/tax-benefit simulation labor supply elasticities"], "reasoning_summary": "For primary earners (typically men or household heads), the labor supply literature generally finds modest substitution elasticities, often in the range of 0.1\u20130.4 on the intensive margin. CBO and similar microsimulation models typically calibrate primary-earner substitution elasticities in the 0.15\u20130.30 range for middle-to-upper deciles. I center my estimate at 0.20, reflecting that decile 7 workers have relatively inelastic labor supply but still respond to marginal wage changes on the intensive margin. Uncertainty spans from near-zero (very inelastic) to about 0.45 (if the model incorporates broader taxable-income responses)."}

citations: Gruber and Saez (2002) - Elasticity of taxable income estimates

runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners in mid-to-upper deciles (decile 7) typically have moderate substitution elasticities. The literature on taxable income elasticities and labor supply for prime-age primary earners generally finds compensated/substitution elasticities in the range of 0.1–0.4, with central estimates around 0.2–0.25. CBO and similar microsimulation models often use substitution elasticities for primary earners that are modest, reflecting the relatively inelastic labor supply of this group. Decile 7 earners are solidly middle-to-upper-middle income, where labor supply responses tend to be smaller than for lower-income workers but still positive. I center my estimate at 0.20, with uncertainty spanning roughly 0.05 to 0.45 to reflect variation across model specifications and parameter calibrations.

runs.reasoningSummary: For primary earners in the middle-upper part of the earnings distribution (decile 7), the substitution elasticity in a tax-benefit microsimulation context is typically modest. The broad labor supply literature finds primary earners (mostly prime-age males) have compensated elasticities in the range of 0.1–0.4. Decile 7 earners are solidly middle-class, not at the top where taxable income elasticities may be higher due to avoidance channels, nor at the bottom where extensive-margin responses dominate. CBO and similar microsimulation models often use substitution elasticities around 0.15–0.30 for this group. I anchor on approximately 0.2 as the median, with moderate uncertainty spanning roughly 0.05 to 0.45 to account for differences across model specifications and calibration choices.

I center my estimate at 0.2 with moderate uncertainty reflecting variation across model specifications and empirical estimates.
